medications.drug_name: mechanical haemostasis (firm pressure ± in-person silver-nitrate cautery) and protective dressing
medications.indication_rationale: Control the bleeding with firm sustained pressure and a protective non-adherent/alginate dressing; in-person cautery if needed. Keep the dressing dry and intact for a few days.

medications.drug_name: definitive excision / biopsy-guided treatment — via GP/dermatology
medications.indication_rationale: Definitive management of BCC is excision (or other modality) after histological confirmation, arranged non-urgently.
Do not advise stopping aspirin unilaterally; the bleeding is controllable with pressure, and aspirin was prescribed for a cardiovascular indication — any change is for the prescriber.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the dressing dry and intact for ~3 days; pat (don't rub) the face when drying; do not pick or scratch the lesion.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Allows a stable clot over fragile tumour vessels and prevents re-bleeding.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a slow-growing skin cancer called a BCC — it is locally destructive but very rarely spreads inside the body. The bleeding is from fragile surface vessels, not a sign of rapid spread.
It needs to be confirmed with a small biopsy and then removed by your doctor within a week or two — important, but not an emergency.
If the dressing soaks through and blood keeps dripping despite 20 minutes of firm pressure, nursing staff should arrange transfer to the emergency department.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Biopsy/excision of the lesion, full skin check, wound healing; nursing dressing changes in the interim.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Bleeding not controlled by 20 minutes of firm pressure, or rapid change/darkening of the lesion — seek urgent/emergency care.

must_include_items: Recognise likely BCC while keeping melanoma/SCC on the differential
Mechanical haemostasis (firm pressure, protective dressing) and note the aspirin bleeding effect
Calibrated reassurance (BCC rarely metastasises) with an uncontrolled-bleeding safety-net
acceptable_alternatives: Punch biopsy vs referral for excisional biopsy
In-person cautery vs pressure/alginate for haemostasis
errors_of_commission: Emergency oncology escalation for a slowly bleeding BCC (over-triage)
Advising the patient to stop aspirin without prescriber input
Declaring it definitely benign/BCC with no biopsy
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires proportionate haemostasis and calibrated reassurance PLUS a definite biopsy/excision pathway that still guards against melanoma/SCC.
